zones.name: Hench Agent

insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected

Perfect cohesion (1.0) with zero external coupling confirms that hench is a well-bounded execution-tier package — all 155 files form a single tight community with no stray cross-package imports leaking out.

The rex-gateway.ts pattern (8 re-exported functions) is the only surface through which hench touches domain-tier state; auditing that no leaf file in src/agent/ or src/store/ bypasses this gateway is the key enforcement point.

At 155 files hench is the largest single-package zone in this batch; if Louvain begins splitting it in future runs (e.g. store vs agent sub-communities), that is a healthy signal to introduce explicit sub-zone pins rather than resisting the split.

Cohesion of 1.0 and coupling of 0.0 are ideal for an execution-tier package: hench depends downward on rex (via gateway) but presents no surface that higher-tier code imports, correctly reflecting its position in the four-tier hierarchy.

Run transcripts are written to .hench/runs/ which is outside the source tree; confirming that the runs directory has a documented retention policy prevents unbounded disk growth in long-running deployments.

The adaptive.ts and review.ts analysis modules alongside spin.ts suggest a multi-phase analysis loop; if these grow further, extracting them into a named sub-zone (e.g. hench-analysis-pipeline) would improve navigability without breaking the package boundary.

The 3 downward imports from core into support suggest utilities were logically promoted to core status but physically left in support — the fix is to either move those 3 utilities up into src/ or extract them into a shared foundation file rather than reaching down.

llm-provider-core acts as a hub with two independent spoke zones (support and tests); this is a sound layered pattern as long as the hub does not import from its spokes — the 3 reverse imports into support break this invariant.

The interface between llm-provider-core and llm-provider-tests is clean and one-directional (25 test imports, 0 reverse) — this is a well-defined boundary and should be preserved as a model for the support layer.

config.ts is the sole origin of all 3 bidirectional edges: api-provider.ts, cli-provider.ts, and create-client.ts each import only from config.ts in the support zone — moving config.ts to the core zone would eliminate the cycle in a single file move with no other changes required.

create-client.ts calls resolveApiKey from config.ts directly rather than accepting a resolved key as a parameter — the factory hard-codes a config resolution side-effect, making unit testing without filesystem fixtures impossible and coupling the factory to the config layer.

codex-cli-provider.ts resolves configuration inline (resolveCodexModel, codexConfig parameter) without using the shared config.ts utilities, while cli-provider.ts delegates to resolveCliPath — two CLI providers use divergent config resolution strategies within the same zone.

create-client.ts imports resolveApiKey from config.ts (support zone) rather than accepting a pre-resolved key — factory functions should receive resolved values via parameters, not perform I/O side-effects internally. This couples the factory to the filesystem and makes isolated unit testing require real config fixtures.

codex-cli-provider.ts and cli-provider.ts implement divergent config resolution strategies: cli-provider.ts delegates to resolveCliPath from config.ts while codex-cli-provider.ts resolves config inline via a passed codexConfig parameter. The inconsistency means the two CLI provider implementations cannot be substituted interchangeably and will diverge further as config logic evolves.

createLLMClient() in llm-client.ts returns ClaudeClient rather than LLMProvider — the top-level vendor-neutral factory bypasses the generic provider interface it was designed to unify, so callers typed to LLMProvider cannot use createLLMClient() without a cast.

Duplicate vendor dispatch exists across provider-registry.ts and llm-client.ts: both contain independent if-else branches on the 'codex' vendor name that must be kept in sync when new vendors are added. provider-registry.ts is the designated extensibility point, but llm-client.ts maintains a parallel hard-coded dispatch that circumvents the registry.

suggest.ts and help-format.ts export pure CLI presentation utilities (Levenshtein distance, color formatting) with no imports from any provider or LLM-related module, yet they are first-class exports of the foundation package. They are in the core zone by file placement but have no conceptual relationship to LLM provider infrastructure.

createLLMClient() return type is ClaudeClient instead of LLMProvider — change the return type annotation so the vendor-neutral factory returns the generic interface. createClient() already returns ClaudeClient & LLMProvider, so only the llm-client.ts wrapper needs updating.

llm-client.ts hardcodes a codex branch independently of ProviderRegistry — callers adding a third vendor must update both files. Remove the manual dispatch from llm-client.ts and delegate to defaultRegistry.getActiveProvider() to make the registry the single extension point.

suggest.ts and help-format.ts are CLI presentation utilities with no LLM logic — their presence in the public.ts barrel inflates the package's surface with non-LLM concerns. Consider grouping them under a documented 'CLI utilities' section in public.ts or extracting to a subpath export (e.g. @n-dx/llm-client/cli) to signal the different role.

12 entry points combined with being the minor partner in a 3+23 asymmetric cross-zone coupling suggests src/ is functioning as a public API surface layer — the wide entry-point count is structurally consistent with an intentional public API zone rather than an architectural defect

AuthMode (types.ts) and ProviderAuthMode (provider-interface.ts) are parallel auth-mode types serving the same semantic role but with different extensibility: AuthMode is a closed union ('api' | 'cli') while ProviderAuthMode is open-ended. The OPENAI_API_KEY environment variable stripping in codex-cli-provider.ts is an undocumented external dependency: if OPENAI_API_KEY is present in the environment, it overrides Codex CLI session credentials, causing auth failures that are silently retried with the key removed. AuthMode (types.ts) and ProviderAuthMode (provider-interface.ts) are duplicate auth-mode types — AuthMode should be replaced with ProviderAuthMode or aliased to it so ClaudeClient.mode and ProviderInfo.mode share the same type. The OPENAI_API_KEY retry-without-key workaround in codex-cli-provider.ts embeds an undocumented environmental assumption about Codex CLI credential precedence — document this behavior in a JSDoc block or inline comment at the point where CodexConfig is defined in llm-types.ts so downstream consumers know the env interaction exists.

insights: Isolated files — no import edges between 4 files, cohesion is unmeasurable (reported as 0)

Cohesion and coupling are both 0.0 — expected for a data-only zone with no import edges. This zone is correctly isolated from source code.
prd.json is the only file with concurrent-write risk (noted in CLAUDE.md); no locking mechanism exists, so consumers should treat it as eventually consistent under concurrent plan/work runs.
workflow.md is human-readable state documentation — keeping it co-located with the machine-readable prd.json is a good discoverability pattern.

The absence of any write-locking mechanism for prd.json is not just a concurrency documentation gap — it is an implementation gap: sync-engine.ts and sync.ts in analyze-engine write to this file, and no atomic-write protocol (temp-file + rename, advisory lock, or write journal) is enforced at the call site. The CLAUDE.md warning exists at the operator level but nothing prevents a library consumer from writing incorrectly.

prd.json has no enforced atomic-write contract at the implementation level. sync-engine.ts and sync.ts write to it directly with no temp-file-and-rename or advisory lock, making the concurrent-write risk documented in CLAUDE.md a latent data-corruption bug rather than an operator caution.

execution-log.jsonl has no documented or enforced size cap — in long-running projects with frequent hench runs, the file will grow unbounded. Unlike prd.json (which is rewritten) or workflow.md (which is overwritten), jsonl files only accumulate lines, making unbounded growth the default behavior with no trim mechanism visible in the codebase.

workflow.md is a human-readable artifact while config.json, prd.json, and execution-log.jsonl are all machine-readable. Co-locating mixed-audience artifacts in the same zone conflates documentation state with operational state — workflow.md serves a different reader (human operators) than its zone siblings (AI tooling, dashboards).
execution-log.jsonl has no size cap or rotation mechanism. Add a trim-to-N-lines step after each hench run write to prevent unbounded growth in long-lived projects.

workflow.md (human-readable) is co-located with machine-readable operational files. Consider whether it belongs in a separate documentation artifact or if its regeneration contract should be explicitly documented alongside prd.json.

mcp-tools.ts mixes static and dynamic import of the same module: TOOL_VERSION is statically imported at the top while REX_DIR is lazily loaded via await import() inside a function body — inconsistent import strategies within a single file are a reliable indicator of an underlying circular dependency that was patched with a dynamic import workaround rather than resolved structurally

TOOL_VERSION and REX_DIR both reside in src/cli/commands/constants.ts — a CLI command file — yet TOOL_VERSION is consumed by MCP service infrastructure (mcp.ts, mcp-tools.ts) and REX_DIR by 20+ files across all zones; these two constants serve entirely different purposes and their co-location in CLI command infrastructure creates artificial cross-layer coupling

mcp-tools.ts uses a dynamic await import('./commands/constants.js') for REX_DIR inside a function body while statically importing TOOL_VERSION from the same file at the module top-level. This inconsistency indicates a circular dependency that was patched with a lazy import rather than resolved by extracting shared constants to a foundation module. The static import path and dynamic import path must be reconciled.

mcp-tools.ts imports 20+ items directly from core domain modules (tree, stats, delete, next-task, transitions, timestamps, parent-completion, dag, cascade-reset, move, merge, verify, reorganize, reorganize-executor, health, facets) with no gateway/facade layer. Every other cross-package consumer in the monorepo uses a gateway; the absence of one here makes mcp-tools.ts the highest-risk refactoring surface in the package.

REX_DIR is defined in src/cli/commands/constants.ts and imported by 20+ files spanning every zone in the codebase. Every non-CLI zone that imports REX_DIR is forced to depend on CLI command internals solely to read a directory path string.

The dynamic imports in mcp-tools.ts serve two structurally distinct purposes that should not be conflated: (1) legitimate lazy-loading of optional LLM features (reshape-reason.js, reason.js, project-config.js — loaded only when mode !== 'fast') and (2) a cycle-break workaround for REX_DIR from constants.js.
